questions.question: What do Anthropic's RSP, OpenAI's Preparedness Framework, and DeepMind's FSF have in common?
options: They are identical documents
They are cousin artifacts addressing the same question — when should a frontier lab pause or gate a model — converging on long-range autonomy, deceptive behavior, and an internal review body
They are all legally binding regulations
They all forbid autonomous agents entirely
questions.explanation: All three track long-range autonomy, name deceptive behavior (alignment faking, sandbagging) as a risk class, have a Safety Advisory Group, and publish standing artifacts on a cadence — while diverging on how categories trigger mitigations.

questions.question: In OpenAI's Preparedness Framework v2, what is the key difference between Tracked and Research Categories?
options: Tracked are public; Research are secret

Tracked Categories trigger Capabilities + Safeguards Reports reviewed before deployment; Research Categories (including Long-range Autonomy) are monitored but do NOT automatically trigger mitigations — language is "potential"
Research Categories are higher risk than Tracked
Tracked Categories apply only to image models
questions.explanation: The hinge is which bucket a capability lives in: Tracked means gated, Research means observed. Long-range Autonomy sitting in Research is the specific delta to watch — it's tracked but not gating.

questions.question: How does DeepMind's FSF v3 treat autonomy differently from OpenAI?
options: It ignores autonomy

It folds autonomy into the domains where it causes harm (ML R&D and Cyber) rather than naming it as a top-level category — e.g. ML R&D autonomy level 1 = fully automate the AI R&D pipeline at competitive cost
It bans autonomous models
It treats autonomy as identical to cyber risk
questions.explanation: DeepMind's argument: autonomy without a domain is capability without risk. The counter-argument: cross-domain autonomy is a meta-risk the framework should name. April 2026 added Tracked Capability Levels for granularity.

questions.question: Why does sandbagging complicate all three frameworks at once?
options: It makes models run slower
If a model strategically underperforms on evaluations, every framework's capability thresholds are underestimated — the framework only works if the measurement works
It only affects open-weights models
It is illegal under all three policies
questions.explanation: Sandbagging is in OpenAI's Research Categories, addressed via Anthropic's eval-context gap and DeepMind's deceptive-alignment monitoring. Because it undermines measurement itself, external evaluation (METR, Lesson 21) and adversarial evals are necessary beyond lab self-assessment.

questions.question: What ceiling do the frameworks themselves acknowledge about monitoring-based defenses?
options: Monitoring is sufficient forever
DeepMind states automated monitoring for instrumental-reasoning misuse "will not remain sufficient long-term" if instrumental reasoning strengthens
Monitoring catches 100% of attacks
Monitoring is banned by the EU AI Act
questions.explanation: Even the labs concede monitoring-only defenses have a ceiling — the same theme as the classifier and kill-switch lessons. This is why the four policy-reading checks (locate, classify, cadence, independence) and external measurement matter.
